data.title: Microsoft Stock Is 'Likely Range Bound,' for Now, Analyst Says. It's All About Capex. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Angela Palumbo

Microsoft stock won't be taking off anytime soon as investors digest heavy artificial-intelligence spending and decelerating cloud revenue, says a Stifel analyst.

Brad Reback lowered his price target on Microsoft stock to $475 from $515, while maintaining a Buy rating. The new price target implies a 20% increase from Wednesday's closing price of $396.89, compared with the 30% increase implied in the analyst's previous price target.

Reback wrote in a research note that he believes the stock "is likely range bound until the market gains comfort that Azure/Commercial Cloud growth can sustainably run ahead of capex increases."

Capital expenditures have been closely monitored by Wall Street as major tech companies commit to spending big on AI. Microsoft said in January that it plans on investing about $80 billion on AI initiatives in 2025. The tech giant then reported second-quarter capex of $15.8 billion, which rose from the previous quarter's $14.9 billion. Meanwhile, revenue in the second quarter from Azure and other cloud services increased by 31%, which was down from 33% one quarter earlier.

Reback wrote that investors are waiting to see revenue for Microsoft's cloud business grow faster than the growth in capital expenditures, "much like it did during the fiscal 2017-fiscal 2023 time frame before management began the current genAI capex ramp."

Microsoft stock has fallen 11.4% since the company announced earnings on Jan. 29. It's been 28 trading days since then. According to Dow Jones Market Data, this is the worst performance for such a stretch following earnings since May 2010, when the stock slid 18%.

For starters, Microsoft provided disappointing third-quarter revenue guidance on Jan. 29. Investors are also waiting to see when Microsoft can turn its heavy spending into revenue growth. On top of those concerns, the overall sentiment on Wall Street has turned sour amid rising uncertainty over the economic environment. Highly valued tech stocks specifically have taken a hit as investors look to mitigate risk.

Still, Reback has confidence in Microsoft's long-term potential.

"We continue to expect the rapidly emerging genAI cycle to allow Microsoft to sustain double-digit revenue and profitability growth for years to come, but lower our target to $475 based on the higher capex assumptions," he said.

Shares of Microsoft were down 1.2% on Friday to $392.20.

Write to Angela Palumbo at angela.palumbo@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: AmFam P&C combined ratio improves 14.2 points to 96.6% in 2024
data.description:
(The Insurer) - American Family Insurance said on Wednesday that its P&C combined ratio improved by 14.2 percentage points to 96.6% in 2024, while company-wide revenue increased by $2.9 billion year on year to $20.0 billion.

The Madison, Wisconsin-based mutual, which is led by chair and CEO Bill Westrate, reported a 3.9 point improvement in its expense ratio to 33.1%.

AmFam swung to a P&C net underwriting gain of $603 million in 2024, compared with an underwriting loss of $1.7 billion in the prior year.

The improved 2024 result came even though AmFam suffered $3.3 billion of catastrophe claims. In 2023, AmFam had reported $3.5 billion of catastrophe claims.

“Multiple severe weather events continued in 2024, and American Family helped its customers recover from damaging tornadoes, hurricanes, hail and thunderstorms with straight-line winds across numerous states,” the company said.

AmFam paid out an additional $8.2 billion in non-catastrophe claims in 2024.

The $2.9 billion year-on-year growth in revenue across AmFam’s operations was driven by rate actions and an increase in investment income, the company said.

AmFarm’s direct premiums written increased 13.9% year on year to $19.6 billion in 2024.

Policy retention and new business declined by 2.8% from 2023, with AmFam ending 2024 with 13.6 million policies in force.

As the company explained, that reduction came as it continued to focus on its core business segments, while it also executed profitability improvement plans in response to natural catastrophes, inflationary pressures and other external forces.

In its life insurance business, policies in force grew by 0.6%. American Family Life Insurance Company reported a gain from operations of $129 million for 2024, down from the prior year’s $150 million.

Company-wide, AmFam’s members’ equity grew by $2.6 billion year on year to $10.6 billion. AmFam said the increase was due to improved underwriting results, investment income and the sale of non-standard auto-focused The General to Sentry Insurance.

AmFam’s group assets increased to $42.2 billion in 2024, up from 2023’s $38.0 billion.

“Sharpening our focus on customer value and accelerating strategic actions helped strengthen our financial position in 2024 and established a solid foundation and momentum to build upon,” said Westrate.

“Our customers are at the center of all we do, and financial strength ensures we’re able to deliver on our promises to serve and support customers whenever they need us most,” he added.

data.title: USDA: Canada Halts Importation of Meat Products From Smithfield Foods Facility in North Carolina
Gap's Winning Look: Celebrity Moments and a Tight Belt — Heard on the Street — WSJ
data.description:
By Jinjoo Lee

Creative leadership looks good on Gap. It looks even better with a tight belt on.

The clothing company, which also owns Old Navy and Banana Republic, posted some impressive results.

  • Its namesake brand's comparable sales rose 7% last quarter from a year earlier, much higher than expected and marking the fifth consecutive quarter of growth.
  • Old Navy and Banana Republic's comparable sales rose 3% and 4%, respectively, also beating expectations by a wide margin.

Shares jumped 13% Friday morning.

On the earnings call, Chief Executive Richard Dickson said product newness and compelling marketing, particularly on social media, helped lift Gap sales. He said new customer visits rose 25% to Gap's website over the holidays, when it partnered with young TikTok music artists for a gift campaign. Old Navy's holiday campaign featured Jennifer Hudson.

Ever since the company hired Zac Posen as its creative director, the brand has been able to draw high-profile collaborations. Most recently, Gap outfitted Timothée Chalamet with a Bob Dylan-inspired outfit at an Academy Awards nominee dinner. The brand also launched a spring campaign with Parker Posey.

Importantly, while Gap is rebuilding its cultural cachet, it is also growing its cash pile, some of which is being returned to shareholders.

  • For the fiscal year, Gap spent $5.1 billion on operating expenses, $100 million below the prior year.
  • Operating margins have risen for eight consecutive quarters, helping Gap post net income growth of 11% last quarter, when Wall Street was expecting a decline.
  • The resulting free cash flow helped Gap's year-end cash balance swell 38% to $2.6 billion, the highest in 18 years.
  • Gap also made its first share buyback last quarter since 2022, and has about $400 million remaining under its repurchase authorization.

Gap thinks it can find another $150 million of cost savings for the this fiscal year, which will include cuts across technology, marketing, overhead and store expenses.

Fashion can be fleeting, but financial discipline has lasting appeal.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: ALX Oncology Is Maintained at Hold by Stifel
Trump administration views bitcoin reserve as "digital Fort Knox", senior officials say
data.description:
Senior officials of the administration of U.S. president Donald Trump said in a media briefing on Friday that they view bitcoin as deserving "special treatment" as the White House forges ahead with plans to create stockpiles of digital assets.

Trump signed an executive order on Thursday creating a strategic bitcoin reserve that the officials described as "a digital Fort Knox for digital gold."

Fort Knox is the United States Bullion Depository, traditional home of a large part of the country's gold reserves, which takes its name from the nearby U.S. Army installation.

The order also created a separate digital assets stockpile, to be governed under different rules. Such a move would address some of the concerns voiced by industry participants ahead of a White House crypto summit on Friday about the prospect of creating a single multi-coin strategic reserve.

"Bitcoin is special in our view," said one of the senior administration officials. "It's the original cryptocurrency. It's the most secure; it's never been hacked. It's scarce; the bitcoin protocol limits the number (of coins) to 21 million. It's the most decentralized."

The executive order also allows Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick "to develop budget-neutral strategies for adding to that reserve."

Just as presidential administrations throughout history have treated gold as a strategic asset to be held indefinitely without worrying about price fluctuations, so the administration will not be concerned about short-term movements in the price of bitcoin, officials said.

The rules are different for the digital asset stockpile, which will hold positions in cryptocurrencies other than bitcoin acquired by the U.S. through any forfeiture proceedings. The Treasury may not purchase additional holdings for this stockpile.

"The principle there is responsible stewardship, and so the secretary of the Treasury has the discretion to make adjustments to that portfolio or adjust that portfolio, as long as it is budget neutral," an administration official said.

A group of senior crypto industry leaders is scheduled to meet with Trump and other administration leaders, including David Sacks, the president's crypto policy adviser, for a four-hour-long roundtable discussion starting at 1:30 PM ET on Friday at the White House.

data.title: Canada Unemployment Rate Steady at 6.6% in February — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--Snowstorms and tariff uncertainty left Canada's job market at a standstill last month, with hiring stalling and the unemployment rate holding steady below its recent peak.

If not for the salvo of tariffs launched by the U.S., the static employment picture suggests the odds lean toward the central bank pausing its aggressive cycle of interest rate cuts next week, particularly in light of recent signs of an economic pickup and a slight acceleration in inflation. Instead, economists expect data will start showing signs of stresses brought on by escalating trade threats, leaving the door open for borrowing costs to keep falling.

Employers in the country added just 1,100 jobs in February, after filling roughly 210,000 jobs over the previous three months, Statistics Canada said Friday. That lack of movement came as the country's labor force notched its first meaningful decline since mid-2022 and meant the unemployment rate was unchanged at 6.6%, or 5.6% using U.S. Labor Department methodology.

Economists were expecting about 20,000 jobs to be added to the economy for the month and for the unemployment rate to tick up after edging lower the previous two months from a high of 6.9% in November.

There were signs of softness in the data, though the government agency noted its survey was conducted when severe winter storms buried parts of central and eastern Canada.

Average hours worked were down by the most since April 2022, declining 1.3% from the month before. Statistics Canada said 429,000 employees lost work hours in February, more than four times higher than the average time lost due to weather for the same month over the previous five years.

The snow may also have contributed to dampened labor market participation last month. The proportion of the working-age population who were either employed or unemployed slipped 0.2 percentage point to 65.3%, the first decline since last September as a marginal rise in participation among core-age workers was countered by falls for young workers and those 55 and older.

Still, President Trump's intensified focus on trade with Canada and threats of tariffs since taking office are already weighing on measures of business confidence and may be showing in hiring.

Nathan Janzen, assistant chief economist at Royal Bank of Canada, said that on an industry basis hiring in the goods sector ticked lower in February, including a pull-back in employment for the heavily trade-exposed manufacturing sector that partially retraced a surge in jobs during the prior two months. Employment in transportation services, which is also heavily trade-exposed, fell by about 23,000 but saw an offset from an about 51,000 jump in jobs in more domestically-focused wholesale and retail jobs.

The job market had shown signs of firming in recent months, but economists worry businesses will be reluctant to add to their ranks with the uncertainty of a trade war with the U.S. and could begin laying off workers if demand in the U.S., Canada's biggest export market by far, slumps.

Offering a possible hint at hesitancy, Canadian job postings on Indeed.com dipped in February after rising in December and January.

The Bank of Canada has lowered its policy interest rate six times since June as inflation has decelerated back toward its 2% target, but at the last meeting in late-January it left out any guidance on the path of rates given the uncertainty of U.S. tariffs. Analysts widely expect another cut Wednesday after Trump pushed ahead with tariffs on imports from Canada earlier this week, even if he days later paused levies on some goods.

Tariffs on Canadian exports, and Ottawa's retaliatory measures aimed at the U.S., are expected to push up prices in both countries and drag heavily on Canada's growth prospects. Even beyond the tariff threat, federal government efforts to slow immigration that has greatly swelled the population in recent years is expected to affect the job market in Canada, and potentially soften economic growth that was stronger than expected in the last quarter of 2024 and into January.

Growth in Canada's working-age population in February was less than half that recorded 12 months earlier and the lowest since April 2022, and the labor force in the country shrank by 16,800, the first meaningful drop since mid-2022."Whether or not February's stall in hiring is the first crack caused by tariff uncertainty or simply the product of data volatiity, it is likely that further weakness will be seen in the months ahead due to those ongoing trade tensions," said Andrew Grantham, senior economist at CIBC Capital Markets.

Grantham, like other economists, expects the central bank will lower its benchmark rate a further quarter percentage point Wednesday. "While the Bank of Canada can't solve the tariff issue with lower interest rates, it can help the economy transition towards other growth drivers."

Statistics Canada's labor force survey showed overall job gains last month came from part-time work, with employment rising 20,800 to offset a 19,700 decline in full-time employment. The private sector drove gains, while employment in the public sector and among the self employed was little changed.

"Looking through the monthly wobbles, it's reasonably clear that the job market had been turning the corner in recent months...until the trade war erupted," Bank of Montreal's chief economist Douglas Porter said.

Write to Robb M. Stewart robb.stewart@wsj.com

data.title: Update: Ensign Energy Down 9% as It Swings to Q4 Loss on Lower Revenue
data.description:
(Updates shares.)

Ensign Energy Services on Friday reported that it swung to a loss in the fourth quarter as revenue fell.

The oilfield-services company said it lost $20.2 million, or $0.11 per share, in the period, compared to a profit of $31.9 million, or $0.17, in the year-prior quarter.

Revenue declined to $426.5 million from $430.5 million.

Adjusted EBITDA came in at $113.4 million, or $0.62 per share, down from $129 million, or $0.70 per share.

Ensign said it plans to cut debt this year by $200 million. Total debt reduction from 2023 to 2025 is expected to be about $600 million.

The company's shares were last seen down $0.22 to $2.23 on the Toronto Stock Exchange.

data.title: Broadcom's Fiscal Q1 Results Should Provide Relief Amid AI Anxiety, Morgan Stanley Says
data.description:
Broadcom's fiscal Q1 results should provide relief to investors amid concerns over artificial intelligence market conditions, Morgan Stanley said in a note Friday.

The company reported fiscal Q1 non-GAAP earnings of $1.60 per diluted share, up from $1.10 a year earlier, as revenue rose to $14.92 billion from $11.96 billion.

Morgan Stanley highlighted AI networking as a key driver, with Broadcom's Ethernet AI segment growing 40% quarter-over-quarter. AI revenue totaled $4.1 billion, exceeding the company's prior guidance of $3.8 billion, and is expected to grow to $4.4 billion next quarter.

However, recovery in non-AI semiconductor segments remained slow, with non-AI revenue down 9% sequentially.

The investment firm also noted the potential of Broadcom's application-specific integrated circuits or ASIC in AI as the company disclosed two potential new customers for the chips, adding to 3 confirmed customers and 2 previously disclosed prospects.

Morgan Staley raised its price target on Broadcom's stock to $260 from $246, while maintaining an overweight rating.

Shares of Broadcom were up 3% in recent trading.

data.title: Yescoin founder arrested in Shanghai following dispute with business partner, company says
data.description:
Zoroo, the pseudonymous founder of the web3 mini-game Yescoin, has been reportedly taken into Shanghai police custody following a dispute with his business partner. 

"What began as a business agreement between partners has now escalated into a criminal case," Yescoin wrote in a Friday social media post. "As members of the web3 community, we deeply regret this incident, which undermines consensus and peace. We are saddened by the disruption caused by this event."

It added, "We would like to assure everyone that Yescoin continues to operate normally." 

Yescoin is a "swipe to earn" game built on The Open Network (TON), a blockchain ecosystem associated with Telegram's messaging platform. To play Yescoin, users swipe their finger across their screen to collect tokens and bonuses, and can complete additional tasks to earn more rewards. Players can then escalate throughout different league tiers, with each league giving users access to more valuable coins. 

The Block previously reported that in August 2024, the Yescoin team said it was planning to launch a token at a time when its game had 30 million users. 

The Block reached out to Yescoin for comment. 

Disclaimer: The Block is an independent media outlet that delivers news, research, and data. As of November 2023, Foresight Ventures is a majority investor of The Block. Foresight Ventures invests in other companies in the crypto space. Crypto exchange Bitget is an anchor LP for Foresight Ventures. The Block continues to operate independently to deliver objective, impactful, and timely information about the crypto industry. Here are our current financial disclosures.

© 2025 The Block. All Rights Reserved. This article is provided for informational purposes only. It is not offered or intended to be used as legal, tax, investment, financial, or other advice.

data.title: Measles cases rise to 30 in New Mexico, state health department says
data.description:
The New Mexico health department reported 30 measles cases in the state on Friday, an increase of 20 cases from its previous count.

All the cases were reported in Lea County, that is located adjacent to Gaines County, Texas, where more than 100 cases and one death in an unvaccinated child have been reported.

data.title: Update: Algonquin Power & Utilities Q4 Adjusted Earnings Drop, Miss Estimates
data.description:
(Adds comment and updates shares.)

Algonquin Power & Utilities on Friday said its fourth-quarter adjusted earnings fell, missing analyst estimates.

Adjusted earnings, which exclude most one-time items, dropped to US$45.2 million, or US$0.06 per share, from US$81.3 million, or US$0.12, in the year-prior quarter. The consensus estimate among analysts polled by FactSet expected $0.09 per share.

Revenue was US$584.8 million, down from US$588.2 million. Adjusted EBITDA declined to US$248.6 million from US$262.1 million.

National Bank of Canada reiterated its outperform rating and US$6.25 price target on the company's shares after the news.

"Although Q4 results were a little soft, we believe investor attention will be focused on the outlook for 2025 and the continued progress on cost and revenue optimization," National Bank said.

The company's shares were last seen down $0.40 to $6.43 on the Toronto Stock Exchange.

data.title: Trump Administration Weighs Pulling Hundreds of Millions in Federal Funds From Columbia — WSJ
data.description:
By Liz Essley WhyteMatt Barnum and Douglas Belkin

The Trump administration is discussing pulling hundreds of millions in federal contracts and grant commitments to Columbia University as part of the government's efforts to penalize schools it believes aren't doing enough to combat antisemitism.

The Ivy League school has come under scrutiny by the Trump administration for an alleged failure to protect Jewish students. The grants in question are from the Department of Health and Human Services, the General Services Administration and the Department of Education.

A person with knowledge of the discussions said the university will have a 30 day review period to address the government's concerns and potentially reinstate the contracts.

The administration was aiming to cut a much higher number of grants, worth billions together, but decided today to keep intact some National Institutes of Health grants to Columbia, a person familiar with the matter said. Columbia received more than $639 million in grants from the NIH, a division of HHS, in 2024, according to government data.

The administration announced a review of Columbia's contracts and grants earlier this week. The agencies said at the time that they were considering stop-work orders for $51.4 million in contracts with Columbia and a "comprehensive review" of more than $5 billion in grant commitments to the school.

On Friday, Education Secretary Linda McMahon was in New York City and sat for interviews with Fox News and Newsmax. "The president has said he's absolutely not going to allow federal funds going to these universities that continue to allow antisemitism," McMahon said on Fox. "To allow this kind of unrest — this is not about freedom of speech."

The Justice Department has said it is sending a task force to visit Columbia and a group of other universities that have dealt with antisemitism. Those schools had some of the biggest pro-Palestinian demonstrations that disrupted campuses last year and prompted rebukes from across the political spectrum.

Columbia drew especially heated criticism from some alumni for what they perceived as the university's tepid response.

The protests led the school to move classes online in April. A month later Columbia canceled its main graduation ceremony, and in August, President Minouche Shafik resigned 13 months into the job.

Columbia has said it is committed to combating antisemitism and all forms of discrimination.

Write to Liz Essley Whyte at liz.whyte@wsj.com, Matt Barnum at matt.barnum@wsj.com and Douglas Belkin at Doug.Belkin@wsj.com

data.title: EnQuest Confirms Potential Reverse Takeover Deal With Serica Energy
data.description:
EnQuest on Friday acknowledged Serica Energy's earlier announcement confirming talks for their potential business combination.

The British energy company said the possible merger will be carried out by way of a reverse takeover in which it will make an all-share offer for Serica.

EnQuest added that there is still no certainty that it will make an offer.

data.title: AM Best assigns Enstar subsidiary Cavello Bay A rating
data.description:
(The Insurer) - AM Best has assigned a financial strength rating of A and a long-term issuer credit rating of “a-plus” to Enstar subsidiary Cavello Bay Reinsurance Limited.

Cavello Bay, a Class 3B reinsurer, is Enstar's primary non-life run-off consolidator.

The ratings, which have been given a stable outlook, reflect Cavello Bay’s "very strong" balance sheet strength. The ratings also reflect its strong operating performance, favourable business profile and appropriate enterprise risk management.

As a part of Enstar's 2024 acquisition by Sixth Street, AM Best has reviewed the group’s prospective capital actions and believes its balance sheet strength should remain at the current level throughout the close of the transaction and the remainder of 2025.

Enstar CFO Matt Kirk said the rating reflects the company's "established standing in the global legacy market and is further confirmation of our strong capital position and the resilience of our business model".

"The 'A' rating for Cavello Bay, our primary Bermuda reinsurer, affirms our commitment to insurance ratings and will enhance our ability to structure insurance transactions that support the strategic objectives of our partners," he added.

Should Enstar’s balance sheet strength deviate materially from its provided plans, AM Best said it would revise the ratings of Cavello Bay.

Enstar's Sixth Street acquisition, which was “overwhelmingly” approved by its shareholders in a $5.1 billion deal, is expected to close in mid-2025.

Enstar offers capital release solutions through its network of group companies in Bermuda, the US, the UK, Continental Europe, Australia, and other international locations. It has acquired more than 120 companies and portfolios since its formation in 2001.

data.title: Brazil grain trader Agribrasil talks with suitors, sources say
data.description:
Brazilian listed grain trader Agribrasil has held talks with suitors interested in buying a stake in the firm, according to two sources familiar with the matter speaking on condition of anonymity because the discussions are private.

Two sources named Solaris Commodities, an independent commodity trader headquartered in Dubai, as one of the potential suitors. One source cited interest from Asian investors and named the Saudi Agricultural and Livestock Investment Company (SALIC), based in Riyadh, as a potential suitor.

No agreement has been reached, according to one of the people.

A deal would give Agribrasil, one of the few independent grain traders in Brazil, access to fresh capital to grow the business.

From a foreign buyer's viewpoint, closing a deal would represent setting foot in the farm powerhouse nation or grow their presence here, as the world grapples with trade wars and nations strive to guarantee food security.

Solaris and SALIC did not have an immediate comment. Agribrasil said as a listed company, information is only shared through the officials channel of the local stock market regulator, without elaborating.

Agribrasil traded in 1.858 million tons of soybeans and corn in the first nine months of 2024, according to the latest available financial information. That represents a 68% rise from the same period in the previous year, driven mainly by a large increase in third-party soy traded volumes shipped through TESC, a port terminal in Santa Catarina state.

Exports of soy and corn accounted for 84% of Agribrasil's sales in the nine months ending in September 2024, according to financial disclosures.

The company owns 51% of TESC.

data.title: GMS Is Maintained at Hold by Truist Securities
data.description: (16:37 GMT) GMS Price Target Cut to $80.00/Share From $97.00 by Truist Securities

data.title: Boeing Faces Rising Costs and Supply Chain Risks as Tariffs Take Hold
data.description:
Boeing CEO Kelly Ortberg told employees that the Trump administration's new tariffs could significantly raise costs on parts imported from Canada, such as landing gear, and create broader supply chain disruptions, Bloomberg reported Thursday.

Ortberg expressed concern that tariffs could become a "continuity of supply issue," making it costly for Boeing if critical components become unavailable. A 25% increase in operating costs for aerospace firms could limit their ability to pass higher prices to airline customers, according to aerospace consultant Kevin Michaels.

Ortberg also mentioned that Boeing is collaborating with Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency to accelerate Air Force One deliveries for Trump.

Additionally, he emphasized the need for a more open corporate culture, urging employees to speak up and improve cross-division communication.

Boeing shares have dropped 13.5% over the past month as rising tariffs threaten raw material costs and the company's global supply chain, which includes manufacturing operations in Winnipeg.

data.title: Russian Garantex cryptocurrency exchange targeted in international operation, US says
data.description:
The United States, Germany and Finland have taken down the online infrastructure used by the Russian cryptocurrency exchange Garantex, the U.S. Justice Department said on Friday, adding that two administrators of the exchange were also charged.

The two administrators, Aleksej Besciokov and Aleksandr Mira Serda, are both charged with money laundering conspiracy. Besciokov, a Russian resident and Lithuanian national, faces additional charges of violating sanctions and operating an unlicensed money transmitting business, the department said.

The exchange has processed at least $96 billion in cryptocurrency transactions since April 2019, the department said.

"The U.S. Secret Service has seized website domains associated with the administration and operation of Russian cryptocurrency exchange, Garantex as part of an ongoing investigation," Nate Herring, lead public affairs specialist at the U.S. Secret Service, said in an emailed statement on Friday, providing no additional comment.

Garantex was sanctioned by the U.S. in April 2022. The exchange on Thursday said the stablecoin Tether had blocked digital wallets on its platform, forcing it to suspend operations days after coming under EU sanctions.

Blockchain research company TRM Labs said in a blog post that the takedown of Garantex "marks a major milestone in the fight against illicit finance" but cautioned that sanctioned exchanges often attempt to evade restrictions by creating new entities.

data.title: Scandinavian Astor Group Plans Production Capacity Expansion with New Investments
data.description:
Scandinavian Astor Group (ASTOR.ST) is making new investments for its subsidiaries to expand production capacity, primarily to meet growing demand from the defense industry.

The Swedish defense and industrial group expects its 6 million-krona new etching machine order for its Mikroponent unit to start operations in the summer, according to a Friday release.

Further investments are also planned for Astor's Marstrom subsidiary and its new production facility in Ljungby, Sweden.

data.title: Business First Bancshares, Inc. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Business First Bancshares, Inc., a financial holding company headquartered in Baton Rouge, Louisiana, operating under the brand b1BANK, has released its annual 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces. The company offers a wide range of banking products and services across Louisiana, the Dallas/Fort Worth metroplex, and Houston, targeting small-to-midsized businesses and high net worth individuals.

Financial Highlights

Net Income: $59.7 million, a decrease of $5.9 million or 9.0% from the previous year, reflecting challenges in maintaining profitability despite revenue growth.

Net Interest Income: $227.4 million, an increase of $12.3 million or 5.7% from the previous year, driven by higher interest income on loans and securities.

Earnings per Common Share: $2.27 per basic common share and $2.26 per diluted common share, compared to $2.62 per basic common share and $2.59 per diluted common share for the previous year, indicating a decline in shareholder returns.

Return on Average Assets: 0.86%, down from 1.04% in the previous year, highlighting a decrease in asset efficiency.

Return on Average Common Equity: 9.54%, down from 12.36% in the previous year, showing reduced profitability relative to shareholder equity.

Business Highlights

Business Overview: First Bancshares, Inc. is a financial holding company headquartered in Baton Rouge, Louisiana, operating under the brand b1BANK. It provides a comprehensive range of banking products and services across Louisiana, the Dallas/Fort Worth metroplex, and Houston.

Expansion Strategy: The company focuses on expanding its presence in existing markets and opportunistically entering new markets along the I-10/12 and I-20 corridors, targeting small-to-midsized businesses and high net worth individuals.

Recent Acquisitions: In 2024, First Bancshares completed the acquisition of Waterstone LSP, LLC, and Oakwood Bancshares, Inc., enhancing its service offerings and market presence in Texas.

Competitive Strengths: The company differentiates itself with sophisticated business lending capabilities, a true community banking model, and an entrepreneurial culture that attracts talent from larger institutions.

Operational Platform: Significant investments have been made in risk management and technology to support growth and maintain operational efficiency, allowing for a high-tech/high-touch approach without extensive physical locations.

Lending Activities: The bank offers a variety of business-related loans, including commercial and industrial loans, construction and development loans, and commercial real estate loans, with a focus on small-to-midsized businesses.

Credit Risk Management: The company employs a disciplined credit approval process and active credit risk management, including a watch list for potential credit risks and regular stress testing of the loan portfolio.

Deposit Growth: Deposits are the primary funding source, with a focus on relationship-driven banking and competitive pricing to promote core deposit growth.

Wealth Management Services: b1BANK offers wealth management and private banking services, targeting high net worth individuals and providing a range of financial planning and investment management products.

Human Capital Resources: The company emphasizes diversity, equity, and inclusion, with a focus on recruiting and retaining a diverse workforce. It offers competitive compensation and benefits, and invests in employee development and training.

Regulatory Environment: The company operates under a complex regulatory framework, with compliance to federal and state banking regulations being a critical aspect of its operations.

Future Outlook: First Bancshares aims to leverage its competitive strengths and operational platform to capitalize on growth opportunities within its existing footprint and in new markets, while maintaining a disciplined approach to expansion and risk management.

Strategic Initiatives

Growth through Acquisitions: The company pursued growth through acquisitions, including the acquisition of Waterstone and Oakwood, which expanded its presence in Texas and enhanced its SBA lending services. Additionally, the company sold its Leesville banking center to streamline operations and focus on core markets.

Capital Management: The company increased its total shareholders' equity to $799.5 million, primarily due to the acquisition of Oakwood, which resulted in stock issuance of $103.8 million. It also maintained a strong capital position with Tier 1 Leverage, Common Equity Tier 1, Tier 1 Risk-based, and Total Risk-based Capital ratios of 9.53%, 9.44%, 10.56%, and 12.75%, respectively. The company declared quarterly dividends for both preferred and common shareholders and participated in the Federal Reserve's Bank Term Funding Program, repaying $300 million in loans by March 2024.

Future Outlook: The company plans to continue prioritizing organic growth while seeking additional acquisition opportunities. It aims to maintain compliance with regulatory capital standards and expects to manage its growth to remain well-capitalized. The company also intends to explore further capital management strategies, including potential dividend adjustments and share repurchases, to enhance shareholder value.

Challenges and Risks

Geographic Concentration: The company faces significant risks due to its geographic concentration in Louisiana, Dallas/Fort Worth, and Houston, which may magnify the impact of regional economic downturns, particularly in the real estate and energy sectors.

Competition: The company is exposed to competition from larger financial institutions with more resources, which could impair growth and profitability.

Execution and Personnel: The company's success is heavily reliant on its ability to execute its business strategy, attract and retain key personnel, and manage credit risk effectively.

Commercial Real Estate Loan Portfolio: The company faces risks related to its commercial real estate loan portfolio, which could be adversely affected by economic changes impacting real estate values and liquidity.

Interest Rate Shifts: The company is vulnerable to interest rate shifts, which could affect net interest income and overall financial results. The rapid increase in interest rates during 2022 and 2023, followed by a decline in 2024, has created uncertainty in loan demand and deposit competition.

Natural Disasters: The company is exposed to risks from natural disasters, particularly hurricanes, which could disrupt operations and increase credit losses.

Internal Control Environment: Management has identified the need to maintain a strong internal control environment, especially after identifying a material weakness in internal control over financial reporting related to IT systems. The company is working with third-party service providers to address these deficiencies.

Liquidity Risks: The company is focused on managing liquidity risks, particularly given the reliance on alternative funding sources that have become more expensive due to rising interest rates.

Market Risks: The company is exposed to market risks related to interest rate fluctuations, which could impact the fair value of its investment securities portfolio. The company has a significant portion of its assets and liabilities in variable rate instruments, making it sensitive to interest rate changes. The company’s asset-liability management strategy aims to mitigate these risks, but external factors such as governmental monetary policies and economic conditions remain beyond its control.

SEC Filing: Business First Bancshares, Inc. [ BFST ] - 10-K - Mar. 07, 2025
